name: Factors and Multiples — Building Blocks of Numbers
whatIsIt: A FACTOR of a number divides it exactly with nothing left over — factors are the equal-sized building blocks a number can be split into. A MULTIPLE is what you get when you count a number in jumps: the multiples of 4 are 4, 8, 12, 16... A number with exactly two factors (1 and itself) is PRIME; with more is COMPOSITE.
whyNeeded: Factors and multiples are the secret behind sharing fairly, simplifying fractions, finding common time-tables (when two buses meet again), and spotting patterns. HCF (highest common factor) and LCM (lowest common multiple) grow straight out of them.
realLifeUses: Arranging 12 chairs into equal rows — the factors of 12 tell you the ways
Two buses leave together; multiples tell you when they leave together again (LCM)
Sharing sweets into equal packets with none left over
Simplifying a fraction by dividing top and bottom by a common factor

vocabulary.meaning: A number that divides another exactly, with no remainder. 3 is a factor of 12.
The result of multiplying by 1, 2, 3... The multiples of 5 are 5, 10, 15...
A number with exactly two factors: 1 and itself (2, 3, 5, 7, 11...).
A number with more than two factors (4, 6, 8, 9...).
Highest Common Factor — the biggest factor two numbers share.
Lowest Common Multiple — the smallest multiple two numbers share.

story.title: Arranging Chairs for the Function
story.text: Meena must set 12 chairs in equal rows for a family function. 'How many ways can I do it?' Mama shows her: 1 row of 12, 2 rows of 6, 3 rows of 4 — and their turn-arounds. 'Each neat way is a FACTOR pair of 12. The factors of 12 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12 — every number that divides 12 with nothing left over.'
story.extractedProblem: In how many ways can 12 chairs be set in equal rows, and what are the factors of 12?
story.answerInStory: The factors of 12 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12 — giving rows like 3×4 and 2×6 (with turn-arounds).

visual.caption: 12 as a 3 × 4 array — 3 and 4 are factors of 12. Rearrange to see 2 × 6 and 1 × 12 too.

teachingGallery.title: Factors build a number; multiples are its jumps
teachingGallery.note: Factors fit into a rectangle exactly. Multiples are skip-counts.

standardMethod.summary: To find factors, test which numbers divide it exactly; to find multiples, skip-count. Prime = exactly two factors.
steps: FACTORS: try 1, 2, 3... and keep those that divide the number with no remainder — they come in pairs.
PRIME check: if the only factors are 1 and the number itself, it is prime; otherwise composite.
COMMON: shared factors give the HCF; shared multiples give the LCM.

alternateMethods.name: Rainbow factor pairs
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To list every factor without missing any.
steps: Start at 1 and pair it with the number itself.
Move up: 2 and its partner, 3 and its partner...
Stop when the pair values meet in the middle — draw arcs like a rainbow.

alternateMethods.name: Common-multiple meeting
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To find the LCM of two numbers.
steps: Circle the first one that appears in BOTH lists.
alternateMethods.example: LCM of 4 and 6: 4,8,12... and 6,12... meet at 12.

trickPractice.intro: Test big numbers instantly: by 2, 5, 10 (last digit) and 3 (digit-sum).

hintLadder: Divisibility rule for 5: a number works if it ends in 0 or 5.
Divisibility rule for 2: a number works if it ends in 0,2,4,6,8.
Divisibility rule for 10: a number works if it ends in 0.
Divisibility rule for 3: a number works if it digit-sum is a multiple of 3.
Divisibility rule for 5: a number works if it ends in 0 or 5.
Divisibility rule for 2: a number works if it ends in 0,2,4,6,8.
Divisibility rule for 3: a number works if it digit-sum is a multiple of 3.
Divisibility rule for 10: a number works if it ends in 0.

commonMistakes.mistake: Mixing up factors and multiples — calling 24 a factor of 12.
commonMistakes.fix: Factors are SMALLER (they fit inside): factors of 12 are ≤ 12. Multiples are BIGGER (they stretch out): 24 is a MULTIPLE of 12, not a factor.
1 has only ONE factor (itself), not two. A prime needs exactly TWO different factors, so 1 is neither prime nor composite. The smallest prime is 2.

commonMistakes.mistake: Listing factors but missing one of a pair (writing 1,2,3,6 for 12 but forgetting 4 and 12).
commonMistakes.fix: Factors come in PAIRS. Every time you find one, write its partner too: for 12, finding 3 means writing 4 (3×4=12).

easy.q: Which of these is a FACTOR of 10?
easy.explain: 5 divides 10 exactly, so 5 is a factor.

medium.q: How many factors does 6 have in total?

challenge.q: What is the LCM (lowest common multiple) of 4 and 6?
What is the HCF (highest common factor) of 12 and 18?

hintLadder: Which one is in the 5-times table?

questions.explain: First common multiple of 3 and 4 is 12.

teachBackPrompt: Be the number detective! Teach someone at home the difference between a factor and a multiple using 12 — show the factor pairs (arrays of chairs) and the multiples (skip-counting) — and why 1 is not prime.

revisionCard.summary: Factors DIVIDE (fit inside, come in pairs); multiples are SKIP-COUNTS (stretch out). Prime = exactly 2 factors. HCF = biggest shared factor; LCM = smallest shared multiple.

gameMission.title: The Building Blocks
gameMission.brief: Help Captain Count arrange chairs and catch bus timetables — find factors, multiples, and the sneaky primes.

realLifeProject: Find the factors of your house number by trying to arrange that many buttons into equal rows. Which arrangements work?
